summary: An exploration of Simone de Beauvoir's philosophy of love, drawing from her early diaries and published works. It highlights her intrinsic interest in non-monogamy, her critique of marriage and traditional monogamy as stifling to eroticism and freedom, and her redefinition of fidelity as a commitment to mutual freedom and singularity rather than sexual exclusivity.

principles.statement: Love requires recognizing the absolute singularity of the other person.
principles.rationale: Loving someone means treating them as a unique freedom rather than reducing them to a type, a set of qualities, or comparing them to others.
principles.source_quote: I would love each of them as if he were the only one

principles.statement: True love is based on radical mutual generosity and reciprocity, not transactional exchange.
principles.rationale: Reciprocity honors the dual human condition of freedom and facticity, forming the foundation of ethical human relationships.
principles.source_quote: I will take from each all that he has for me and I will give him all that I can give him

principles.statement: Eroticism contains a principle hostile to society.
principles.rationale: Eroticism affirms individuality and draws lovers into a private domain, resisting the public, productive roles imposed by society and institutions like marriage.
principles.source_quote: eroticism is hostile to society because it involves an engagement of singularity over and beyond social roles

mental_models.name: Necessary vs. Contingent Love
mental_models.description: A hierarchical model for open relationships where a primary, long-term partnership is deemed 'necessary' while allowing for secondary, 'contingent' love affairs.
mental_models.example: Beauvoir and Sartre's famous pact, agreeing to a primary partnership while remaining free to pursue other lovers.

mental_models.name: Fidelity to Ambiguity (Situated Transcendence)
mental_models.description: Redefining fidelity not as sexual exclusivity, but as faithfulness to the human condition—recognizing both oneself and the other as free subjects who change over time.
mental_models.example: Choosing to part ways with a lover if staying together requires one to be 'unfaithful to what one is.'

heuristics: Lovers should part when staying together would require one party to be unfaithful to what one is.

signature_quotes.text: I would love each of them as if he were the only one I will take from each all that he has for me and I will give him all that I can give him who can reproach me for it
signature_quotes.context: From her 1929 diary, reflecting on loving two men simultaneously.

signature_quotes.text: between us it is a matter of a necessary love it is suitable that we also know contingent loves
signature_quotes.context: Describing the pact made with Jean-Paul Sartre in 'The Prime of Life'.

signature_quotes.text: it will be necessary to have a never-ending faith and the value of the one from love and know only with faith because she is different from oneself one must love him in this very difference
signature_quotes.context: Describing her concept of fidelity to the other's freedom.

anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It commits individuals to lifebound contracts that fail to acknowledge the self's process of becoming, stifles eroticism by making the relationship a public institution, and historically traps women without reciprocity.
It involves the passionate abandonment of oneself and one's freedom, which paradoxically seeks to control the other person rather than allowing lovers to walk side-by-side as equals.
